metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that child 02cd wires `LLMConfig.timeout_s` to the actual pydantic-ai `run_sync()` call (e.g. via `inactivity_timeout` kwarg or equivalent SDK mechanism), not merely stores it in config — confirm the mechanism against the pydantic-ai API surface before landing.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' (child 02cd), but the current runner.py wires cfg.timeout_s into LLMConfig yet passes NO timeout argument to any of the three `agent.run_sync()` call sites — pydantic-ai surfaces per-run timeout via an `inactivity_timeout` kwarg on `run_sync` (not via ModelSettings), so the mechanism by which the configured timeout_s reaches the SDK is unverified and likely absent.
findings.location: Success Criteria / 'The LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default (child 02cd)'; src/rebar/llm/runner.py lines 318, 456, 469
findings.suggested_fix: In PydanticAIRunner.run(), pass `inactivity_timeout=cfg.timeout_s` (or the pydantic-ai equivalent, e.g. `model_settings={'timeout': cfg.timeout_s}` if that is the correct key for the pinned version) to each `agent.run_sync()` call site. Pin a test that a FakeRunner/TestModel call with a short timeout raises within that budget.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split child c3f8's triage AC into a separate follow-on task created at stack-merge time (or add an explicit 'open a triage ticket on first CodeQL run' step), so the pre-merge AC covers only 'workflow file exists, runs green, permissions correct' and the post-merge triage has its own trackable home.
findings.finding: Child c3f8's acceptance criterion 'Any true-positive findings from the first run are triaged (fixed or dismissed with a reason) before the ticket closes' cannot be verified in CI before the stack merges to main, because the first CodeQL run only executes after the workflow file lands on main. This bundles a post-merge manual action into a pre-merge AC, creating a dependency gap where the ticket formally cannot be closed until an asynchronous, unscheduled activity completes.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise c3f8's AC to: (1) pre-merge: workflow exists, permissions are least-privilege, SHA-pinned, schedule configured; (2) post-merge: first-run triage is a separate child ticket created upon stack merge, linked discovered_from c3f8, closed only after Security tab is clean.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence description of 'staged-phantom reset' to the Success Criteria or Context section (e.g. 'a staged write's temp files cannot survive a reset/interrupted commit and be picked up by a subsequent write') so the AC line is grounded in described work.
findings.finding: 'staged-phantom reset' is named as a reliability deliverable in the Acceptance Criteria but is never described or introduced anywhere else in the plan body (not in Context, Success Criteria, or Sequencing Note), leaving the implementer no way to verify what work this covers.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — reliability children line: 'staged-phantom reset'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria section, add a bullet: 'A staged-write phantom (leftover temp file from an interrupted commit) cannot be silently reused by a subsequent write; the git-store resets staged state on startup or error recovery.' Then the AC line maps to this described deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In child 02cd (or the epic body), specify which pydantic-ai API surface carries cfg.timeout_s (e.g. httpx Timeout on the provider object, asyncio.wait_for around run_sync, or a model_settings timeout key) so the implementation decision rule is co-located with the LLMConfig schema that holds the value.
findings.finding: The plan's timeout-wiring criterion (child 02cd) specifies the OUTCOME ('honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default') but does not specify the pydantic-ai API surface that must receive cfg.timeout_s. Tool-grounded reading confirms cfg.timeout_s is never passed to any agent.run_sync() call site in runner.py (lines 318, 456, 469), yet pydantic-ai exposes timeout via multiple incompatible paths (model-level HTTP client settings, model_settings dict, or an asyncio timeout wrapper) — the decision rule for which path is co-located neither with the LLMConfig schema that holds timeout_s nor with the child story.
findings.location: Success Criteria + Acceptance Criteria (reliability children) — LLM timeout wiring; runner.py lines 318, 456, 469

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure (e.g., revert commit recipe or feature flags) for the runtime-behavior-changing children (LLM timeout, unmapped-status guard, push-merge lock) so a regression can be undone without a full stack revert.
findings.finding: The plan lands all runtime-behavior changes (claim-race surfacing, push-merge lock, LLM timeout wiring, unmapped-status guard) as a single Gerrit stack onto main with no feature flags, canary phase, or explicit rollback procedure.
findings.location: Sequencing note / Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: For each behavior-inverting child, add a one-line rollback note (e.g., 'revert commit SHA X; no data cleanup required') and, for the unmapped-status guard specifically, consider a SHORT_CIRCUIT env-var that restores silent-reopen during a burn-in period.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the target SDK name and its default timeout value (or the floor value) in the LLM-timeout success criterion so the AC is self-contained.
findings.finding: 'The LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' leaves 'SDK default' undefined — the plan does not name the SDK or its default timeout value, requiring the executor to investigate before they can verify compliance.
findings.location: Success Criteria — LLM timeout bullet; also referenced as 'child 02cd'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the bullet to read: 'The LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the <SDK name> SDK default of <N> seconds (child 02cd).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'mypy strictness ratchet started' with a specific observable end state, e.g., 'pyproject.toml [tool.mypy] section enables at minimum <list of flags> and CI fails on any new mypy error above the baseline of <N> errors.'
findings.finding: 'mypy strictness ratchet started' is effort-framed and not a specific observable end state — 'started' does not define what configuration or error-count baseline constitutes completion.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Typing and config-surface ratchets' bullet; AC — 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start…) landed'
findings.suggested_fix: State the minimum set of mypy flags enabled (e.g., `disallow_untyped_defs = true`, `warn_return_any = true`) and the CI enforcement mechanism (e.g., `mypy --error-summary` exit code gates the build) so the criterion is binary pass/fail.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'docs are navigable by humans' with a specific, verifiable outcome, e.g., 'docs/index.md exists and links to every top-level docs file; CI runs a broken-link check (e.g., markdown-link-check) that passes.'
findings.finding: 'docs are navigable by humans' is a subjective criterion with no measurable definition — there is no observable artifact or deterministic check that confirms navigability.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Docs drift called out by the audit is fixed' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Success Criteria bullet or a Sequencing Note entry for '.joe-janitor/.tokensave hygiene' that states the expected post-landing state (e.g. 'files removed from repo root and added to .gitignore') so the AC line is verifiable.
findings.finding: '.joe-janitor/.tokensave hygiene' is named as an OSS/CI deliverable in the Acceptance Criteria but is absent from both the Success Criteria and the Sequencing Note, so there is no described expected outcome to verify the AC against.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — OSS/CI children line: '.joe-janitor/.tokensave hygiene'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Success Criteria: 'Repo-root hygiene files (.joe-janitor, .tokensave) are removed or gitignored so they do not ship in distributed tarballs or leak local state into CI artifacts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a feature flag, a config option to revert to passthrough mode, or an expand-contract sequence) for the unmapped-status guard behavior change, so operators have a documented path if the guard causes regressions.
findings.finding: The unmapped-status guard explicitly stops previously-allowed behavior ('can no longer silently reopen local tickets'), which is a consumer-visible behavior change with no rollback/back-out plan described. Any workflow that implicitly relied on the passthrough will break with no documented migration or expand-contract sequence.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Inbound Jira statuses that have no mapping can no longer silently reopen local tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config knob (e.g., `JIRA_UNMAPPED_STATUS_ACTION=warn|reject`, defaulting to `warn` initially) with a planned cutover to `reject` after one release cycle, giving operators a rollback path; document the cutover timeline in docs/concurrency.md or a short ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit back-out plan for the push-merge write-path locking change (e.g., an emergency bypass config, the specific commits to revert, or a feature flag) so operators can recover if the locking mechanism causes production write failures.
findings.finding: The push-merge write-path locking ('No git-store write path can commit another write's leftovers or race the push-retry merge') introduces a new concurrency mechanism on an existing write path. This is an irreversible architectural change in production behavior, and no back-out plan is described if the locking implementation introduces deadlocks or write starvation.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'No git-store write path can commit another write's leftovers or race the push-retry merge'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit doc artifact (e.g., a section in docs/configuration.md or a short ADR) for the env-var registry: what it is, how to register a new env var, and the canonical list of supported vars, referenced from the drift-gate CI job.
findings.finding: The env-var registry is a new config surface and architectural pattern introduced by the plan, but no user-facing doc or ADR for the registry schema (what env vars exist, their types, defaults, and semantics) is explicitly called out — only the CI drift gate artifact (test.yml) is mentioned in the sequencing note.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Maintainability children (mypy ratchet start, env-var registry + drift gate) landed'; Sequencing note — 'test.yml (0f21 drift gate, d13e 3.13 matrix)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (in child 02cd's spec or AC) that the LLM timeout is surfaced as a configurable parameter (env-var or config key registered in the env-var registry) rather than a hardcoded constant, so operators can tune it and SDK-default changes don't silently under-cut the floor.
findings.finding: The LLM timeout value is asserted as 'no lower than the SDK default' but the plan does not specify whether the wired timeout is a hardcoded constant or a configurable parameter (e.g. env-var / config key). LLM timeouts are an operations-tunable threshold that changes across deployment environments and evolves with SDK upgrades; if hardcoded, future tuning requires a code change rather than configuration.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'The LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default (child 02cd)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the LLM timeout as a named entry in the env-var registry introduced by the 0f21 child (e.g. LLM_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS), with a runtime floor check: effective_timeout = max(config_value, SDK_DEFAULT). Document the floor semantics in the env-var registry table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate any reconciler paths or integration tests that currently observe the silent-reopen behavior from unmapped Jira statuses, and confirm they are covered by the regression test or explicitly migrated.
findings.finding: The unmapped-status guard prevents silent reopen of local tickets, but the plan does not acknowledge whether any existing Jira integration, reconciler workflow, or downstream consumer currently depends on the silent-reopen behavior that is being removed.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Inbound Jira statuses that have no mapping can no longer silently reopen local tickets'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single bullet under the unmapped-status guard child listing known callers of the old code path and state whether they are updated, deprecated, or unaffected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive mechanism description for how the cross-clone claim-race collision is surfaced to the losing claimant (e.g. post-push fork-re-read returning ConcurrencyError, a manifest alert, or a separate fsck-style check), and confirm it does not violate the 'push must not nest inside the write lock' invariant documented in push_after_commit.
findings.finding: The cross-clone claim race 'surfaced to the losing claimant' mechanism has a hidden ordering assumption: claim_core commits and returns success to the caller before push runs, so any fork detection can only happen after push+reconverge — at which point the call stack has already returned. The plan names the outcome ('surfaced to the losing claimant') but specifies no mechanism for how the post-commit fork is propagated back (synchronous push-then-re-read, caller-polling, or alerting). Without this, the child implementer must invent the notification contract, risking either a reentrant lock violation (nesting push-re-read inside the already-released write lock) or a silent detection path that never actually surfaces to the caller.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'The store's cross-clone claim race is surfaced to the losing claimant and pinned by a two-clone test'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify the detection path: after push_after_commit merges origin, re-read the ticket's compiled status under a read (not write) lock; if the local commit's UUID is not the winner (status is in_progress but the winning STATUS uuid differs from the one just committed), raise ConcurrencyError with the same exit-10 contract. Document this as the 'post-push fork check' in the child.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the push-merge lock approach that addresses the 'push must not nest inside the write lock' invariant — e.g., whether a separate merge-phase lock is used, whether the merge is moved inside a write-lock re-acquisition, or whether the lock is relaxed for the push-retry path with documented tradeoffs.
findings.finding: The plan's 'push-merge lock' child goal has a mechanism tension with the existing documented invariant that the push must not run inside the write lock, but the plan provides no positive rationale for how the child resolves this tension. The push_tickets_branch function explicitly runs fetch+merge outside the write lock (confirmed in push.py:168-228), and push_after_commit explicitly documents 'must not nest inside the write lock.' The plan names 'push-merge lock' as the mechanism but gives no rationale for how locking the merge segment is consistent with (or supersedes) this invariant.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Reliability children (claim-race surfacing, push-merge lock, staged-phantom reset, LLM timeout wiring, unmapped-status guard) landed with regression tests'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm before landing child c3f8 that the GitHub mirror repo has GitHub Advanced Security enabled (or is public) and that the codeql.yml workflow will be granted `security-events: write` — document this prerequisite in the child's implementation notes.
findings.finding: The plan's CodeQL child (c3f8) says 'CodeQL runs on the mirror' and CONTRIBUTING.md documents `.github/workflows/codeql.yml` uploading SARIF to GitHub Security → Code scanning, but no codeql.yml exists in `.github/workflows/`. Uploading SARIF via `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif` requires `security-events: write` Actions permission, which is absent from all existing workflows (gerrit-verify.yaml: `contents: read, actions: read`; test.yml: `contents: read`; mirror-guard.yml: `contents: read`). This is an unverified capability assertion about whether the GitHub mirror repo has GitHub Advanced Security enabled and whether the workflow will be permitted to upload.
findings.location: Sequencing note / 'CodeQL first-run triage (c3f8) is performed after the full stack lands on main'; Acceptance Criteria / 'OSS/CI children (CodeQL workflow...)'; CONTRIBUTING.md line 387

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in child 0f21's description whether it extends the existing `MCP_ENV_VARS` / `check_server_manifest.py` drift gate or introduces a new registry for a distinct env-var surface (e.g. CLI/library vars), and update the epic success criterion to name the scope boundary.
findings.finding: The plan introduces an 'env-var registry with a CI drift gate' (child 0f21) as a new maintainability deliverable, but an env-var registry + CI drift gate for the MCP server's environment variables already exists: `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` runs in `test.yml` as the 'server.json env-contract drift gate', keyed on `rebar.mcp_server.MCP_ENV_VARS` as single source of truth. The plan does not distinguish whether child 0f21 extends this existing gate, replaces it, or adds a second, orthogonal registry — risking duplication or confusion about which registry is authoritative.
findings.location: Success Criteria / 'env-var registry with a CI drift gate'; .github/workflows/test.yml line 271; scripts/check_server_manifest.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that child c3f8's `codeql.yml` must declare `permissions: security-events: write` (required by `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif` to post findings to the Security → Code scanning tab) — add this as an explicit auth/permission precondition in the child's acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: The CodeQL workflow (child c3f8) uploads findings to GitHub's 'Security → Code scanning' tab via `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif`, which requires the `security-events: write` Actions permission. The plan names no auth/permission precondition for this workflow, and every existing workflow in the repo is scoped to `contents: read` only. If the new `codeql.yml` omits `security-events: write`, the upload step will fail at runtime with a 403, and Code scanning results will never appear.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'CodeQL runs on the mirror'; Sequencing note — child c3f8; CONTRIBUTING.md line 387–389
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child c3f8's AC or workflow scaffold: `permissions:\n  contents: read\n  security-events: write` — this is the minimum grant needed for `github/codeql-action/analyze` (which internally calls upload-sarif). Also note that if the GitHub org has 'Restrict Actions permissions' enabled, an explicit org-level allowance for `github/codeql-action` may be needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the numeric value of the pydantic-ai SDK's own default timeout (e.g. httpx default, or None/unlimited) in the child-02cd story body so the 'no lower than SDK default' floor is a concrete, verifiable constraint.
findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default' references 'SDK default' as a numeric floor for the child-02cd timeout constraint, but neither the plan nor the codebase defines what the pydantic-ai SDK's own default timeout is. The plan executor implementing child 02cd cannot verify the 'no lower than SDK default' rule without knowing this value.
findings.location: Success Criteria section — 'The LLM gates honor a wired timeout no lower than the SDK default (child 02cd)'
findings.suggested_fix: In child 02cd, state explicitly: 'pydantic-ai/httpx uses a default read timeout of X seconds (or no timeout); cfg.timeout_s (default 600) must be passed to the agent/HTTP layer so it is always >= X.' This grounds the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Coordinate the final post-composition wording for docs/api-stability.md between 2440 and d13e: 2440 should specify exactly what the non-literal replacement text is, and d13e should be written as a delta on that text so the combined result satisfies both ACs simultaneously.
findings.finding: docs/api-stability.md is edited by both child 2440 (remove hardcoded version literal) and child d13e (update supported-version wording to match 3.13 matrix), but neither child specifies the final post-composition wording, creating a risk that d13e re-introduces a version-specific literal that 2440 just removed, or that 2440's removal leaves d13e with nothing to anchor its update on.
findings.location: child 2440-01e4-9cc3-480c and child d13e-e1c3-8bb8-40c4
findings.suggested_fix: Have child 2440 replace the hardcoded version with a stable formulation (e.g., 'See the PyPI project page for the current supported versions'). Then have child d13e only touch README.md and pyproject classifiers for version accuracy, explicitly excluding docs/api-stability.md since 2440 already made it version-agnostic. Update d13e's AC to reflect this narrowed scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step in child 3e8b (or 0f21) to determine whether REBAR_FORCE_MKDIR_LOCK is still read in src/: if live, add a doc-comment so 0f21's generator captures it in the registry; if dead, remove the os.environ read as part of 3e8b's doc cleanup.
findings.finding: REBAR_FORCE_MKDIR_LOCK is an owned-by-none gap: child 3e8b removes the CI claim about it from docs/concurrency.md, and child 0f21 generates a registry of REBAR_* vars actually read in src/ — but neither child audits or acts on whether REBAR_FORCE_MKDIR_LOCK is live or dead in the source. If it is still read in src/, 3e8b's doc correction leaves an undocumented live knob; if it is dead code, it should be removed from src/ but no child is scoped to do so.
findings.location: child 3e8b-033c-6cd4-4711 and child 0f21-ca03-f063-4562
findings.suggested_fix: In child 3e8b, add to its What/Scope: 'grep src/ for REBAR_FORCE_MKDIR_LOCK; if found, add an inline doc comment for 0f21's generator to capture; if not found (dead code), remove the dead read as part of this change.' This closes the owned-by-none gap within the existing Gerrit stack.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
